TypeScript example 0.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a loop.
The example uses a typed array and a number[] value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_0(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_0([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```ts
function process_typescript_0_40(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_40([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```ts
function process_typescript_0_80(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_80([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.

The example uses a typed array and a Record<string, number> value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_238(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_238([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```ts
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_278([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```

The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.

```ts
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_794([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.

TypeScript example 914.
Goal: teach how to filter a collection with a command handler.
The example uses a record and a string value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_914(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_914([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```

```ts
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_993([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a record and a Promise<void> value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_1072(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_1072([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a record and a ReadonlyArray<string> value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_1150(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_1150([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```ts
  return total;
```

The example uses a interface and a Record<string, number> value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_1347(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_1347([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

TypeScript example 1464.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a guard clause.
The example uses a interface and a string value.
```ts
function process_typescript_0_1464(items: number[]): number {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_1464([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.

```ts
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_typescript_0_1504([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is type mismatch.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.

```ts
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}
```
